data.title: Alibaba Q3 Is Critical - But Not A Catalyst
data.description:
Q3 FY2026 could be a critical confirmation of earnings recovery in an investment-heavy phase for Alibaba Group Holding (BABA). While the importance of the upcoming earnings is high, I do not view it as a catalyst that will immediately change the trajectory of the stock. Alibaba enters Q3 after several months of consolidation, but following significant repricing in the months preceding this consolidation phase.

The long-term thesis remains intact, as Alibaba’s investments in cloud infrastructure, AI capacity buildout, and quick commerce position it strongly for structural growth and ecosystem monetization. However, despite management commentary in Q2 indicating that the trough of quick commerce investments should be behind us in Q3, capex spending could continue to weigh on free cash flow and near-term profitability.

More importantly, valuations already assume a recovery in the second half of the year. Even positive surprises may simply allow the share price to grow into current valuation levels rather than trigger a fresh rerating. At the same time, a Sell is not warranted, as cloud revenue is accelerating strongly and demand signals remain robust. Quick commerce losses have also been reduced materially from July/August 2025 levels. All in all, Q3 becomes a critical quarter, but one that doesn't need urgent positioning against. I recommend a Hold, with no fresh positions or trimming activity around Q3. Alibaba's thesis will need at least a couple of quarters of directional data to demand action.

Why Stabilization May Not Be Enough in Q3

I see Q2 as the deepest point of the current investment cycle. Which is what makes the upcoming quarterly results important for confirmation. The degree of rebound versus expectations also becomes critical, wherein the next leg of the rally could emerge. Let us understand where we actually are in the investment cycle. Q2 numbers showed an ~49% drop in EBIT YoY. Diluted EPS was down by ~52%, and free cash flow was reported as negative RMB 21.8b (continued downward trajectory as seen in the TTM FCF chart below).

This decline was, of course, deliberate and is explained by a phase of scale expansion in quick commerce and AI infrastructure capacity buildout (importantly, not a demand destruction-led decline). For the upcoming quarter, this becomes the baseline, and this is what a sequential improvement means quantitatively. Alibaba's quarterly EBIT typically goes through large quarterly fluctuations. But purely from a YoY mechanical perspective, Q2 2025 EBIT was ~85% lower than Q2 2024. These are visibly more compressed profitability levels compared to previous troughs of ~$2.5b in the past 5 years.

The EV to forward EBIT is currently at ~22.69x. Since EV is ~$250b, the implied forward EBIT is ~$11b (~$2.5-$3b quarterly run rate). The total EBIT so far in FY2026 already adds up to above $5.5b, leaving a similar ~5.5b EBIT target for Q3 and Q4 that could be what we can call "meets expectations". So, Q3 confirms the path to this recovery and becomes important. But at the same time, even a 3x sequential improvement of EBIT, taking it to ~$2b-2.5b, just makes it on track for FY2026 recovery expectations. That is a high bar.

The odds of a positive surprise of a magnitude that can shape another rally in share prices are quite low, as I have shown above. Even if there are possibilities of an outlier rebound quarter that really delivers shock surprise, I see valuations absorbing much of that short-term signal. If the EV/EBITDA projections point to a 20% better-than-expected trajectory, there is decent scope for the current valuations to shrink to ~18x EV to forward EBIT (valuation levels still above the January 2025 highs).

Another factor that could surprise positively in Q3 is the timing of recovery, if not the magnitude. However, there too, the EPS expectations show a significant rebound already built into Q3 expectations, leaving little scope for extraordinary outcomes that can deliver a surprise large enough to allow share prices to grow after allowing some of the valuation to cool too. The EPS expectation path shows that the bulk of the remaining ~$5.5 EBIT responsibility lies with Q3, as Q4 is likely to suffer relatively due to seasonal and timing effects.

Strengths That Mitigate Downside Risks

While the near-term likelihood of another leg of rally in Alibaba looks weak, the short-term floor looks protected. In Q2, Alibaba delivered 34% growth in cloud (external customer revenue growth of 29%). AI-related products continued to grow at a triple-digit pace (ninth consecutive quarter), now accounting for over 20% of external cloud revenue. Management also indicated that demand for AI compute continues to outpace supply, with servers running at full utilization and capacity expansion constrained primarily by supply chain limitations rather than customer demand.

Although I have highlighted that Q3's rebound confirmation is important, I find several trends already pointing to a strong rebound. Strong cloud growth is one of the trends that support a likely strong Q3. On the consumption side too, China e-commerce CMR grew 10% in Q2, while quick commerce unit economics improved meaningfully—per-order losses improved 50% since July/August. Logistics efficiency gains and higher average order values suggest scale economics are beginning to show up already. These improvements, noted through Q2, are only likely to continue into Q3 and support the rebound expectations (even if the market may require multiple quarters of consistency before rewarding it).

Importantly, Alibaba is building AI across the full stack - from infrastructure and foundation models to direct integration into its core consumer and enterprise platforms. As Qwen gets embedded into Alibaba's ecosystem, it will have scale impacts across millions of users and merchants. This is not limited to internal efficiency gains. On the external side, accelerating AI demand is already driving strong cloud revenue growth. Alibaba’s distribution reach, proprietary models, and infrastructure ownership together create a complete ecosystem at a scale few companies are attempting. That is a long term structural downside risk mitigant in my view.

This Could Be a Long Wait

Alibaba is poised strongly for a rebound in Q3 and into FY2027. The long-term thesis is extremely strong as the AI ecosystem starts showing gains after a period of building. So, the downside looks limited. However, valuations already embed a recovery and have enough cushion to absorb better-than-expected outcomes in the upcoming quarter. That is where the Hold rating emerges.

In my view, that Hold outlook could remain for a while, with plenty of opportunity to initiate fresh longs - not just a wait for Q3. There are several factors that point to a multi-quarter flat period ahead. For one, the capex outlook remains elevated. As per management, although the quick commerce investments could slow, overall capex, especially in AI infrastructure, could grow even further -

[RMB 380 billion] figure we had mentioned previously might be on the small side, certainly in terms of the customer demand that we're currently seeing.

This means free cash flow normalization may lag EBIT stabilization. As shown in the TTM free cash flow trajectory below, investment intensity has materially compressed cash generation. Even if operating margins stabilize, sustained AI infrastructure spending could continue to weigh on cash conversion.

The earnings recovery path is also unlikely to be linear. While Q3 may mark the beginning of stabilization, consensus EPS expectations already embed a volatile and long-drawn recovery trajectory into FY2027. A durable rerating would therefore require several quarters of consistent margin expansion to validate that trajectory - especially because Alibaba's trajectory is fluctuating by default even under normal circumstances.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: REIT Replay: Office REIT Stocks Plummet In Recent Week Amid Growing AI Fears
data.description:
The majority of US REIT indexes climbed during the week ended Feb. 13, outperforming the broader stock market.

The Dow Jones Equity All REIT index jumped 4.04% over the recent week, while the MSCI US REIT (RMZ) index increased 3.31%.

The broader stock market indexes logged weekly declines, with the S&P down 1.39% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average dropping 1.23%.

Nearly all REIT indexes closed the recent week in the black. The self-storage REIT index jumped 6.74%, followed by the healthcare REIT index, which rose 5.79%. The hotel and retail REIT indexes also increased 2.01% and 1.38%, respectively.

The top five performing REIT stocks for the week were populated by data center, information storage and communications REITs. Data center REIT Fermi Inc. (FRMI) recorded the largest share-price increase, 28.63%, among all US REIT stocks with at least $200 million in market capitalization. Information storage-focused Iron Mountain Inc. (IRM) ranked second with a share-price increase of 14.67%, followed by data center REIT Equinix Inc. (EQIX) at 12.74%.

Communication REITs American Tower Corp. (AMT) and Crown Castle Inc. (CCI) rounded out the top five performing REIT stocks with increases of 12.16% and 11.75%, respectively.

While the other REIT indexes logged weekly increases, the office REIT index plummeted during the recent week amid growing fears that AI could lead to fewer white-collar jobs and thus less need for office space.

The Dow Jones US Real Estate Office Index fell 6.72% during the week to 152.40, its lowest level since July 2009.

The four bottom-performing REIT stocks all came from the office sector. Hudson Pacific Properties Inc.'s (HPP) share price plummeted 19.26% during the week, the largest share-price decline among all US REIT stocks with at least $200 million in market capitalization. Cousins Properties Inc. (CUZ) and Highwoods Properties Inc. (HIW) ranked next with share-price declines of 12.40% and 11.11%, respectively, while Kilroy Realty Corp.'s (KRC) share price dropped 9.29% over the week.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Seanergy Maritime: Buy On Strong Results And Promising Outlook
data.description:
Note:

I have previously covered Seanergy Maritime Holdings Corp., or "Seanergy Maritime" (SHIP), so investors should view this article as an update to my previous work on the company.

On Tuesday, leading dry bulk shipper Seanergy Maritime reported decent fourth-quarter results, with both revenues and profitability coming in above consensus expectations:

The company achieved a daily time charter equivalent (“TCE”) rate of $26,614, well above my estimate of $24,500, due to further strengthening Capesize charter rates in the second half of the quarter.

Seanergy Maritime generated a record $26.8 million in cash flow from operating activities. Free cash flow amounted to $24.6 million.

The company ended the quarter with $62.7 million in cash and cash equivalents as well as $290.2 million in debt (net of deferred financing costs). Total debt principal was $294.0 million.

Seanergy Maritime declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.20, which will be paid on April 10 to shareholders of record as of March 27.

Please note that the company's dividend policy gives the board of directors broad discretion over the actual payout level.

While Seanergy Maritime did not buy back any shares during the fourth quarter, the company recently extended its repurchase program by one year to December 31, 2026.

Under the program, Seanergy Maritime may repurchase up to $25.0 million of its outstanding common shares in the open market, with approximately $20.1 million remaining available at the end of Q4.

Subsequent to year-end, the company placed two additional newbuild orders with Chinese shipyards:

Our fleet renewal program is progressing as planned and remains a core strategic priority. In recent months, we added two prompt, eco newbuilding orders at leading Chinese shipyards: a scrubber-fitted Capesize sister vessel to the unit previously announced, scheduled for delivery in Q3 2027, and a scrubber-fitted Newcastlemax scheduled for delivery in Q2 2028.

The total current newbuilding investment of approximately $226 million reflects our intention to continue pursuing selective and prompt newbuilding opportunities when market conditions and financing terms are favorably aligned.

Based on statements made by management on the conference call, the newbuild program is not expected to negatively impact dividend levels going forward.

In addition, Seanergy Maritime agreed to sell the 2010-built Capesize vessel Dukeship to spin-off United Maritime (USEA):

In February 2026, the Company entered into an agreement with United Maritime Corporation (“United”), a related party, for the disposal of the M/V Dukeship through an 18-month bareboat charter.

The charter period commenced following the delivery of the vessel on February 12, 2026. United has advanced a downpayment of $5.5 million and will pay a daily charter rate of $9,450, with a purchase obligation of $22.1 million at the end of the bareboat charter.

As the sale won't be finalized before H2/2027, the company's fleet still consists of 20 vessels with an average age of 14.6 years and an estimated market value of more than $700 million:

Seanergy also provided an outlook for the first quarter, with 77% of available days fixed at an estimated TCE rate of $24,739. Based on recent Forward Freight Agreement ("FFA") rates, management estimates the final TCE rate to be approximately $25,300.

At these rates, the company should easily beat current consensus expectations again.

The near-term outlook for Capesize vessels remains supported by increased levels of bauxite and persistently healthy iron ore shipments in combination with a rather small order book. In addition, the ongoing ramp-up of the giant Simandou mine in Guinea should continue to benefit ton-mile demand.

While the company's share price has benefited from the improved charter rate environment and rising second-hand vessel values, Seanergy Maritime continues to trade at a material discount to estimated net asset value ("NAV"):

While most of the company's publicly traded competitors command somewhat younger fleets and lower leverage levels, Seanergy Maritime remains the only real Capesize pure play besides Himalaya Shipping (HSHP):

Given the combination of discounted valuation and strong Capesize charter rates, I am reiterating my "Buy" rating on the company's common shares.

In addition, I am increasing my price target from $12.00 to $16.00 in order to better reflect strong near-term prospects and assign an NAV discount closer to the industry average.

Risks

Escalating trade tensions could impact demand for key commodities like iron ore, coal, and bauxite and, in turn, charter rates for dry bulk carriers. With China being, by far, the largest importer of commodities, investors will have to keep a close eye on economic conditions in the country.

Bottom Line

Seanergy Maritime Holdings reported better-than-expected fourth-quarter results and raised its quarterly cash dividend from $0.13 to $0.20 per share.

In addition, the company expanded its fleet renewal program by placing two additional newbuild orders with Chinese shipyards while selling a 16-year old Capesize vessel to spin-off United Maritime.

Importantly, the newbuild program is not expected to negatively impact dividend levels.

Given the favorable near-term outlook in combination with the company's discounted valuation, I am reiterating my “Buy” rating on Seanergy Maritime's common shares with an increased price target of $16.00.

data.title: Suburban Propane: Questionable Model That Relies On Debt And The Weather Yet Brings About No Results
data.description:
Suburban Propane (SPH) has been around for a while. But the model on which it is based is another matter. Anytime you see a limited partnership model, most investors think "income". But Suburban Propane distributes natural gas at the retail level. This was covered more in the last article to result in a strong sell. Since the main idea still holds, I am not about to change my stance. This is a very weather dependent business, even though natural gas is often considered a utility by many. Furthermore, as a retailer, there is less benefit from a hot summer than is the case for some of the upstream companies that I follow. That means the earnings emphasis is on the winter. It is a very unpredictable variable distribution entity as a result.

It therefore should not surprise anyone that I recommended this stock after the distribution was cut back around 2018 because of a series of warm winters. Generally, there will be a cold winter sooner or later. As usual, the "buy low" part of the deal is just hard to execute. After all, things will look dire after a warm winter and really not much hope of a significant profit until the next winter. Nonetheless, that idea did not really work out. It was a combination of the weather going forward and a reduction in the market valuation compared to the past.

For an investor, that is a lot of bad things happening at one time that can be prevented by avoiding the whole situation in the first place. The last article spent time on the weather uncertainties. But as shown below, there is more to worry about than just the weather.

Despite management assertions about conservative balance sheet management, that debt ratio is way out of line for a business that is significantly dependent upon the weather. Management can and should control that debt ratio much more than is the case right now (in my opinion). There are way too many variables here that are not producing for the investor.

This business is not to be confused with the typical midstream business where the safety factor is fairly high because business is known well ahead of time and take-or-pay contracts protect the downside. Here, not much protects the downside because a warm winter often means soft prices and low usage.

As management has noted and I have touched on in the last two articles, this is a very fractured market. But that means little to no barriers to entry. That is going to keep margins to a minimum, especially when industry conditions are hostile.

The last article and this article do show a change in the partnership model in that there is less of a tendency to distribute all the cash flow. But realistically, that debt ratio needs to come down a lot. So, there is really a good chance that a conservative decision would be to direct as much cash flow (after taxes) to debt reduction.

But that makes the partnership model next to useless. The whole idea of a partnership model is to pass everything to investors. But keeping the cash while passing the tax liability to investors would be anathema.

Furthermore, this company has a history of making periodic acquisitions (small acquisitions) for as long as I can remember. Yet the gallons sold is not exactly climbing at some sort of growth rate. I would have expected a slide showing cyclical growth as a result of a "rollup" strategy in a highly fractured industry. But that does not appear to be happening either.

Adjusted EBITDA did peak in fiscal year 2022. That should have been expected due to the sky-high commodity prices that prevailed back then. But there is no talk from management about improving the company's performance from the expected recovery of natural gas prices in the future.

Instead, it seems to be more about waiting for better prices to reduce the debt ratio. But that can be a very risky strategy given the low future visibility that prevails in this business.

The first quarter is off to a good start. The second quarter, which is January to March, should also have some very good results, as this La Niña winter has definitely surprised to the cold side. This should be a decent year for the partnership. The problem comes when there is a warm winter or, worse yet, a series of warm winters that caused a discussion in the 2018 article about a distribution cut.

Summary

For me, this is a very strong sell for income investors. I do not view this as an income idea at all. The income predictability in the future is much too low for me. In fact, the very low visibility that prevails in this industry leads me to think that no debt is the way to go. I do not see where the leverage benefits investors at all. If there is no benefit, then the interest paid is basically lost income.

But more importantly, most companies I invest in have some history of growth, even if it is slow growth. Companies like this one that essentially tread water or worse often have an eventual surprise to the downside that I can do without. It seems like slow growth appears to keep the company current and enable a strong competitor over time as well.

The Seeking Alpha website shows this as a competitor whose returns are near the bottom of the pack for 10 years. Frankly, the current year does not show an improvement in the competitive position.

Even if one were to consider a propane distributor, there is bound to be a better long-term choice than Suburban Propane.

The Seeking Alpha Quant System shows this issue as a hold, and it is likely due to the better winter that the company is experiencing. But until there is better evidence of superior performance against the competition, I would strongly advise investors to avoid this.

The return shown above over 10 years is negative. The return in the current year, as shown by the website, is so far lackluster. This is not why investors get involved in an idea like this one.

The good grades in profitability and growth are not translating into investor returns. That is a management issue. That grade that demonstrates that management issue is the momentum.

Revisions due to the weather are good. But they are not what the market is looking for when valuing a business.

In summary, this is the type of situation where I wait for a 5-year history of solid results against whatever competitors you choose (if you do not like the choices that the website made). I personally look for signs of above-average management. But I see no evidence of that here.

Risks

A series of cold winters could result in improved earnings that would push the partnership units to a higher valuation. But this is still a business with a significant dependence on the weather. The future weather has very low visibility.

The other issue is that the market can change in the future how it values earnings. This is what caused the first idea back in 2018 to fail. The earnings before 2018 were valued higher than what came after that (likely at least partially due to the distribution reduction). It could also reverse and cause an unexpected success.

In short, when I do not know how to value something and I see what I consider to be excess debt, then I just avoid it because I cannot tell the outcome of the investment. To me, this idea is closer to gambling than it is investing.

In short, the strong sell is more about what I cannot determine than it is about whether the stock price will go up or down.

The investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. To obtain performance information current to the most recent month-end, which may be higher or lower than the performance shown above, please call 866-667-9231 or go to aberdeeninvestments.com/us/literature.

Returns prior to May 7, 2018 reflect the performance of a predecessor fund. Please consult the Fund’s prospectus for more detail.

Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions.

Total returns may reflect a waiver of part of the Fund’s fees for certain periods since inception, without which returns would have been lower. Indexes are unmanaged and provided for comparison purposes only. No fees or expenses are reflected. You cannot invest directly in an index.

1.59/1.25

1.33/1.00

Expenses stated as of the Fund’s most recent prospectus. All classes of the Fund have contractual waivers in place and may not be terminated before February 28, 2026 without approval of the Independent Trustees.

In terms of individual stock positive contributors, Loma Negra Compania Industrial Argentina (LOMA), the Argentinian cement company, performed strongly after Milei won sufficient votes to continue his reform agenda, having previously sold off in the months leading up to the Argentinian elections amid concerns that he was losing support.

SSE (SSEZY), the UK electric utility, announced an updated capital expenditure (capex) and funding plan that surprised positively. The company raised £2 billion in equity, less than expected, and outlined a £33 billion capex plan through to 2030, higher than expected, which is expected to support 7–9% per annum earnings growth.

RWE (RWEOY), the European electric utility, rose over the quarter as investors grew more optimistic about an AI-driven power market recovery in Europe, similar to what has been seen in the US over the past 18 months. This was supported by greater visibility on planned investment in hyperscale data centres across Europe.

In terms of individual stock detractors, Infrastrutture Wireless Italiane (IFSUF), the Italian tower company, reported disappointing earnings that resulted in a cut to long-term earnings guidance only eight months after it was issued, with management citing reduced visibility from its key customers. One possible driver is a reluctance among customers to commit amid the potential for telecommunications sector consolidation in Italy.

Rumo (RUMOF), the Brazilian rail operator, continued to see weak pricing despite decent volumes, as it faced near-term competitive pricing pressure in some of its key transportation corridors. Separately, its share price was pressured by its largest shareholder, Cosan, selling down two separate 5% tranches of its holding. This reflected liquidity needs at Cosan rather than any fundamental issues at Rumo.

American Tower Corporation (AMT), the US tower company, underperformed as investors reacted negatively to news that EchoStar indicated it would not honour the remainder of its contract with American Tower after the sale of its spectrum. American Tower is appealing the decision, but this is likely to be a protracted legal dispute. For context, the EchoStar contracts represent around 2% of American Tower’s revenues.

Market review

Global equities ended the final quarter of 2025 higher, supported by strong corporate earnings and a couple of 25-basis-point rate cuts from the US Federal Reserve (Fed) over the period. However, sentiment was constrained early in the period by the partial US government shutdown, which delayed key economic data and added to policy uncertainty before a temporary funding bill was agreed in mid-November to run into January 2026. Softer US labour market data and signs of easing inflation later strengthened investor conviction that the Fed could implement further interest-rate cuts in the coming months. Meanwhile, technology companies continued to advance due to optimism about artificial intelligence (AI) despite some volatility in November linked to concerns about valuations in the sector. However, persistent geopolitical tensions surrounding the Russia–Ukraine war and mixed Chinese economic data continued to cloud the global growth outlook.

Outlook & strategy

The focus within energy remains on high-quality midstream businesses with long-term take-or-pay contracts, particularly those exposed to gas. The outlook for gas infrastructure has improved as demand has strengthened, especially in the US, where gas is increasingly important in meeting power needs from AI-related data centres. New commercial opportunities, including integrated power solutions for hyperscalers, are reinforcing the role of gas within the broader energy mix.

In transport infrastructure, roads continue to perform well globally, with traffic volumes generally above pre-pandemic levels and supportive pricing dynamics in the US and Canada. Emerging markets offer growth opportunities, particularly where further privatisation is expected, such as in Brazil. The political environment in France remains a concern, with higher taxation weighing on toll road operators and leading to reduced exposure. Airports remain an area of focus, especially those with a strong leisure travel bias, with opportunities across emerging markets including Argentina, Mexico and Thailand. Rail volumes remain weak, although operational performance in the US has improved.

Within utilities, rising power demand linked to AI remains a central theme, with emphasis on regulated and contracted parts of the sector. In the US, higher capital expenditure is shaping long-term growth expectations, while increased power purchase agreement prices have supported renewable developers. Regulatory dynamics and pressure on customer bills remain key considerations.

In towers, emerging-market exposure performed strongly, reflecting improving financial performance and increasing investor recognition. While some positions have been reduced for risk management reasons, the outlook for African tower operators remains constructive. Developed-market towers continue to face headwinds from higher interest rates and slower telecommunications capital expenditure, with uncertainty persisting in Europe due to consolidation and excess capacity.

Overall, infrastructure continues to stand out as an attractive investment opportunity, providing defensive characteristics and long-term value in an uncertain macroeconomic environment. We also believe infrastructure offers a compelling way to navigate volatility while benefiting from essential, irreplaceable assets. With the focus on near-term trends and political developments, it is important not to lose sight of the long-term structural drivers in infrastructure, namely the energy transition, digital acceleration, ageing infrastructure and increasing urbanisation.

1 The S&P Global Infrastructure Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of stock markets of developed and emerging markets.

For current holdings information, please visit abrdn Global Infrastructure Fund - Portfolio Holdings
